description: The requirement does not define which special characters are permitted or prohibited. Different systems treat characters like spaces, Unicode symbols, or control characters differently. Without an explicit allowed/denied character set, testers cannot deterministically validate compliance, and developers may implement inconsistent rules.

There is no defined maximum password length. This creates a security gap (denial-of-service via extremely long passwords triggering expensive hashing) and leaves acceptance criteria incomplete for upper-bound boundary testing.

The requirement specifies constraints for new password creation but provides no acceptance criteria for how the system behaves when an existing password (set before this policy was enforced) does not meet the current policy — e.g., at next login, at next password change, or immediately.

There is no acceptance criterion specifying the exact error message or user feedback when a password fails validation. Without this, UI behavior is untestable for correctness and consistency across registration and password-change flows.

The requirement does not address whether the password policy also applies to the password-reset and password-change flows, or only to initial registration. This omission creates ambiguity about the scope of enforcement.

There is no requirement for uppercase letter inclusion, password history (preventing reuse), or expiry policy. These are standard security controls whose absence may constitute a security gap depending on the threat model of the application.

The requirement states a verification email shall be sent, but does not specify the trigger event. It is ambiguous whether the email is sent upon initial registration, upon each login attempt before verification, or upon explicit user request to resend. Testers cannot define a clear test entry condition.

No acceptance criteria are defined for what happens when a user clicks an expired link (e.g., error message shown, option to resend, automatic invalidation behavior). The expected system response post-expiry is entirely unspecified.

The requirement does not specify whether the 24-hour expiry is calculated from the moment the email is sent by the server or from the moment it is delivered to the recipient's inbox. These can differ and produce inconsistent test results.

There is no acceptance criterion defining what happens to an unverified account after the link expires — for example, whether the account is deleted, remains in a pending state indefinitely, or triggers a follow-up action. This is a testable state transition that is missing.

The requirement does not specify whether a user can request a new verification link, how many times they may do so, and whether previously issued links are invalidated upon reissuance. This creates a potential security gap where multiple valid links could coexist.

The uniqueness guarantee of the link is not defined in measurable terms. There is no specification of the token generation algorithm, minimum entropy, or format, making it impossible to verify that the link is cryptographically unique and resistant to guessing attacks.

There is no acceptance criterion specifying whether unverified users are permitted to log in (REQ-004) or access any system functionality. The interaction between REQ-003 and REQ-004 is undefined, creating a potential conflict in authentication flow.

The requirement does not specify the content or format requirements of the verification email beyond the link itself (e.g., required subject line, sender address, fallback plain-text version, localization), making completeness testing impossible.

No acceptance criteria defined for what constitutes a successful login. Missing specifics on: what response/redirect occurs after successful authentication, whether email matching is case-sensitive or case-insensitive, session duration/token expiry, and behavior when the account exists but the email is unverified (per REQ-003).

The requirement does not specify whether login is permitted for accounts that have not yet completed email verification (REQ-003). This creates a potential conflict: a registered but unverified user technically satisfies 'registered user' but may or may not be allowed to log in.

The term 'registered user' is ambiguous. It is unclear whether this includes users who are deactivated (REQ-011), users with unverified emails (REQ-003), or users invited but not yet onboarded (REQ-009). Each state may require different login behavior.

No security requirements are stated for the login mechanism itself, such as requirement for HTTPS/TLS, protection against credential stuffing beyond account lockout (REQ-005), password transmission handling, or brute-force throttling at the network level.

The requirement does not specify error message behavior on failed login attempts. Returning distinct messages for 'email not found' vs. 'wrong password' would constitute a user enumeration vulnerability. Acceptable error response content is not defined.

No acceptance criteria exist for session management post-login: session token type, storage mechanism, expiry policy, concurrent session handling, or logout behavior are all unspecified, making the requirement incompletely testable end-to-end.

The requirement does not clarify whether email address matching is case-sensitive. 'User@Example.com' and 'user@example.com' may or may not resolve to the same account, leading to inconsistent test outcomes and potential duplicate account issues.

'Easy and intuitive' is a subjective, untestable statement. No measurable usability criteria (e.g., task completion rate, number of steps, time-on-task benchmark) are provided, making it impossible to objectively verify or write acceptance tests for this requirement.

The use of 'should' (vs. 'shall') makes the obligation level ambiguous. It is unclear whether this is a mandatory requirement or a desirable goal, which affects prioritization and whether non-compliance constitutes a defect.

The requirement does not define what constitutes a 'consecutive' failed login attempt. It is unclear whether the count resets after a successful login, after a fixed time window, or only after the lockout period expires. This ambiguity makes consistent implementation and testing impossible.

There is no acceptance criterion specifying how the 15-minute lockout timer starts (e.g., from the 5th failed attempt or from the last failed attempt). Test cases cannot be written without a precise definition of the timer's start event.

The requirement does not specify whether the user, an administrator, or both should be notified when an account is locked. The absence of a notification rule is a security gap that could allow credential-stuffing attacks to go undetected.

There is no acceptance criterion defining the system's behavior after the 15-minute lockout expires: specifically, whether the failed-attempt counter resets to zero automatically or requires explicit administrative intervention.

The requirement does not address whether an administrator can manually unlock a locked account before the 15-minute window elapses, creating a potential conflict with REQ-011, which grants administrators the ability to revoke access immediately.
The requirement does not define what error message or UI feedback is presented to the user upon lockout or during the lockout period. Without this, the expected user-facing behavior cannot be tested.

The requirement does not specify whether the lockout is enforced per IP address, per user account, or both. This ambiguity could allow attackers to bypass account-level lockouts by rotating IP addresses, representing a security gap.

It is unclear whether the 5-attempt threshold and 15-minute lockout duration are configurable by an administrator or are hardcoded. The absence of this detail makes it untestable whether the system meets organizational security policy requirements that may differ from these defaults.

The requirement states the administrator can 'invite new team members by email' but does not specify what happens after the invitation is sent. There are no acceptance criteria covering: invitation email content, expiry period of the invite link, behavior when the invitee already has an account, behavior when the same email is invited multiple times, whether the administrator can cancel a pending invitation, or what role/permissions are assigned to the invited user upon acceptance.

'New team members' is ambiguous. It is unclear whether this means users who are entirely new to the TaskFlow platform (no existing account) or users who are new only to a specific organization or project but may already have an account. This affects how the onboarding and account-creation flow should behave post-invitation.

The requirement does not define the scope of 'organization administrator'. REQ-010 defines three roles (Administrator, Project Manager, Contributor), but it is unclear whether 'organization administrator' maps directly to the 'Administrator' role in REQ-010 or represents a separate, higher-level role. This creates a potential conflict with the role model defined in REQ-010.

There are no security-related acceptance criteria for the invitation flow. The requirement does not address: whether invitation links should be single-use, whether they should expire (and if so, after how long, contrasting with REQ-003's 24-hour expiry for verification emails), protection against invitation link enumeration or forwarding attacks, or rate limiting on the number of invitations an administrator can send.

The requirement does not specify what role or permission level is assigned to the invited user by default upon accepting the invitation, nor whether the administrator can pre-assign a role at the time of sending the invite. Without this, the interaction with REQ-010 (three distinct roles) cannot be fully tested.

'Easy and intuitive' is a subjective and untestable statement. There is no measurable acceptance criterion (e.g., task completion time, error rate, usability score, or specific UI affordances) that would allow QA to objectively verify this requirement is satisfied.

REQ-013 uses the keyword 'should' (not 'shall'), making it a non-mandatory requirement per standard SRS conventions. However, there is no rationale or condition provided for when it may be acceptable to not meet this requirement, leaving its testability and priority undefined.

The requirement states that Administrator, Project Manager, and Contributor each have 'distinct permissions' but nowhere in the SRS are those permissions enumerated or defined. There are no acceptance criteria specifying what each role can or cannot do, making it impossible to test whether the permission boundaries are correctly implemented.

The phrase 'distinct permissions' is ambiguous. It does not specify whether permissions are additive (e.g., Administrator inherits all Project Manager permissions) or fully disjoint, nor whether permissions are scoped per-project or globally across the organization.

There is a potential conflict between REQ-009, which refers to an 'organization administrator', and REQ-010's 'Administrator' role. It is unclear whether these are the same role or two separate concepts with different scopes (organization-level vs. project-level), which could lead to contradictory permission implementations.

No security controls or access-enforcement mechanism is described. There is no requirement specifying that the system must prevent privilege escalation, enforce role checks server-side, or audit role assignments, representing a significant security gap.

The requirement does not define how roles are assigned, changed, or revoked (e.g., who can assign a role, whether a user can hold multiple roles, and whether role changes take effect immediately or at next login). This missing criteria makes the feature partially untestable.

The requirement does not state whether the three listed roles are the only permitted roles or whether new custom roles can be created. This ambiguity affects both implementation scope and test coverage.

REQ-012 (noted in the header as REQ-12) carries an inconsistent identifier. The document body labels it REQ-012 while the submission header labels it REQ-12, creating a traceability and referencing conflict.

The requirement ID in the header is labeled 'REQ-13' but the document body uses 'REQ-011' and 'REQ-013' for different requirements. The heading 'Requirement REQ-13' appears to reference REQ-011 content, creating a numbering conflict that makes traceability unreliable.

The statement 'the system should make user management easy and intuitive for administrators' uses 'should' (non-mandatory) and subjective terms ('easy', 'intuitive') that cannot be objectively measured or tested. There are no defined usability metrics, task completion benchmarks, or user testing criteria.

No acceptance criteria are defined for what constitutes 'easy and intuitive' user management. Missing criteria such as maximum number of clicks to complete a task, error rate thresholds, learnability benchmarks, or usability test pass/fail conditions.

The scope of 'user management' is ambiguous. It is unclear whether this covers only deactivation (REQ-011), or also invitation (REQ-009), role assignment (REQ-010), profile editing (REQ-012), and any future management features.

The requirement does not specify what happens to active sessions, issued tokens, or in-progress actions belonging to the deactivated user at the moment of deactivation. 'Immediately' is ambiguous with respect to session invalidation, API token revocation, and SSO/OAuth token handling.

No acceptance criteria are defined for 'immediately'. Missing measurable thresholds such as maximum latency for access revocation (e.g., within N seconds), behavior across concurrent sessions, and expected system response if revocation encounters an error.

There is no security requirement addressing audit logging of deactivation events, administrator authorization verification before deactivation, or prevention of an administrator deactivating their own account or other administrators, creating a potential privilege abuse gap.

The requirement does not specify the fate of the deactivated user's owned projects, assigned tasks, comments, or file attachments. It is unclear whether those assets are reassigned, preserved, or become inaccessible, which conflicts with implied project continuity needs.

The phrase 'easy and intuitive' is highly subjective and cannot be objectively measured or tested. There is no defined benchmark, user satisfaction score, task completion rate, or usability metric specified to determine whether the requirement has been met.

No acceptance criteria are defined for this requirement. There is no specification of which user management tasks must be accomplishable, within what number of steps, in what time frame, or with what error rate to qualify as 'easy and intuitive'.

The requirement uses 'should' rather than 'shall', making it a non-binding aspiration rather than a verifiable mandate. This is inconsistent with other requirements in the SRS (e.g., REQ-009 through REQ-012) that use 'shall', creating ambiguity about whether this requirement must be implemented and verified.

The scope of 'user management' is undefined in this requirement. It is unclear whether it refers solely to the actions described in REQ-009 through REQ-012 (invite, role assignment, deactivation, profile update) or encompasses additional undocumented administrative functions, making completeness impossible to verify.

No target user persona or baseline proficiency level for 'administrators' is defined. Without knowing whether administrators are assumed to be technical or non-technical users, the usability standard cannot be consistently designed or tested.
The requirement does not specify maximum or minimum length constraints for the project name and description fields, leaving validation rules undefined and untestable.
No acceptance criteria are defined for what constitutes a valid start date (e.g., cannot be in the past, must be a specific format). It is unclear whether a start date in the past is permitted.

The relationship between the start date and the optional end date is not specified. There is no stated rule preventing the end date from being earlier than or equal to the start date, making validation behavior ambiguous.

The requirement does not define what happens after a project is successfully created (e.g., redirect to project dashboard, confirmation message, notification to team members), leaving post-creation behavior undefined and untestable.

It is ambiguous whether the Project Manager can create a project on behalf of another user or organization, or only within their own organizational scope. The scope of project ownership is not defined.
The requirement does not specify whether project names must be unique within an organization, globally, or not at all. Duplicate name handling is entirely absent.

No access control or security constraint is stated regarding who can view the newly created project after creation (e.g., is it private by default, visible to all org members). This represents a potential security gap.

The requirement does not specify the expected date format or whether the input must respect the user's time zone setting defined in REQ-012, creating a potential conflict with time zone-sensitive scheduling.
REQ-017 appears to be a duplicate or mislabeled wrapper around REQ-014. The requirement ID REQ-017 contains the text of REQ-014, which creates a numbering conflict and makes traceability impossible.
The requirement ID is malformed and inconsistent — it is labeled both 'REQ-18' and 'REQ-015' simultaneously. This makes traceability and cross-referencing unreliable across the specification.

No acceptance criteria are defined for what constitutes a valid 'add' or 'remove' operation. There is no specification of what happens to tasks assigned to a removed member, whether removal is soft or hard, or whether any confirmation step is required.

'Team members' is ambiguous — it is unclear whether this refers only to users already registered in the organization, users with specific roles (e.g., Contributors only), or any arbitrary user. The permissible scope of membership is undefined.

There is a potential conflict with REQ-010 and REQ-011: if an Administrator has distinct permissions, it is unclear whether a Project Manager can add/remove members of any role including other Project Managers or Administrators, or whether role-based restrictions apply.

No notification or audit requirements are defined for add/remove actions. It is unclear whether affected users or administrators should be notified when membership changes occur, creating a gap relative to the notification features described in scope.
There is no defined behavior for removing the last remaining Project Manager from a project. This edge case could leave a project in an unmanageable state and needs explicit handling criteria.

No security constraints are specified regarding whether a Project Manager can only manage members of their own assigned projects or across all projects in the organization, representing a potential privilege escalation gap.

The requirement does not specify the outcome for open or in-progress tasks belonging to a removed team member — e.g., whether tasks are unassigned, reassigned automatically, or blocked. This makes the behavior untestable for those scenarios.

The term 'default project list' is ambiguous. It is unclear what constitutes the 'default' view, whether this means all listing contexts (search results, dashboards, reports, API responses) or only a specific main project list screen, and whether archived projects appear in non-default views.
No acceptance criteria define which user roles are permitted to archive a project. It is unknown whether Contributors, Project Managers, or only Administrators can perform this action.
There are no acceptance criteria specifying how or whether an archived project can be unarchived (restored), who can restore it, or what state the project returns to after restoration.

The requirement does not specify how archived projects behave in search results, reports, and third-party integrations. It is untestable whether 'hidden from the default project list' extends to these surfaces.

There is no acceptance criterion defining whether tasks, comments, file attachments, and associated data within an archived project remain fully accessible, read-only, or restricted in some way after archiving.

The requirement does not address whether team members currently assigned to tasks within an archived project receive notifications, can still be mentioned, or retain any active relationship to the project, creating a potential conflict with REQ-011 (deactivation) and role-permission requirements in REQ-010.
There is no specification of whether a dedicated 'archived projects' view must exist, making the discoverability and recoverability of archived projects untestable.
No security or audit requirements are defined for the archive action, such as logging who archived a project and when, creating a security gap for accountability and compliance.
The requirement does not state whether archiving a project is reversible or permanent, nor define any time-based data retention or deletion policy for archived project data.
The requirement ID stated in the document header is 'REQ-19' while the body text references 'REQ-016', creating an identifier conflict that makes traceability and test mapping unreliable.

The requirement does not specify how the system should handle the case where start date and end date are set to the same day. It is unclear whether start date equal to end date is valid or should also be prevented.

No acceptance criteria are defined for how the validation is triggered (e.g., on form submission, on field blur, in real-time), what error message or UI feedback is presented to the user, or whether the system should reject the save entirely or warn and allow override.
The requirement does not specify which user roles are permitted to set or modify project dates, making it impossible to test enforcement in the context of role-based permissions defined in REQ-010.

The requirement does not address what should happen when an existing project's start date is changed to be later than an already-set end date, or vice versa during an edit operation, leaving the update-flow behavior undefined.

There is no specification of how time zones (referenced in REQ-012) affect date comparison. A start date and end date entered by users in different time zones could yield ambiguous results, creating a potential conflict with REQ-012.

The requirement does not define whether enforcement occurs only on the client side, only on the server side, or on both. Without this, test coverage boundaries cannot be established and a client-side-only implementation would represent a security/integrity gap.
The requirement does not specify behavior when dates are provided via API or bulk import rather than through the UI, leaving a gap in how the constraint is enforced across all entry points.

The requirement ID is inconsistently labeled as both 'REQ-21' and 'REQ-018' in the same statement. This numbering conflict makes traceability unreliable and creates confusion about which requirement is being referenced in test cases, change requests, or defect reports.

The term 'task list' is ambiguous. It is unclear whether this means only the top-level tasks, or also subtasks, task hierarchies, dependencies, and nested structures. The scope of what constitutes the 'task list' must be explicitly defined.

There are no acceptance criteria defining what state the duplicated tasks should be in. For example, should task assignees, due dates, statuses, priorities, and custom fields be copied or reset to defaults? Without this, testers cannot determine pass/fail.

The requirement does not specify what name or identifier the duplicated project should receive. It is untestable whether the naming convention (e.g., 'Copy of [Project Name]', a user-prompted name, or auto-generated) is correct without a defined expected outcome.

There is no acceptance criteria defining the success or failure behavior when the duplication process fails mid-way (e.g., partial copy due to a system error). It is unclear whether a rollback should occur or a partial duplicate is acceptable.

The requirement does not address whether task assignees from the original project are carried over to the duplicated project. If the duplicated project is for a different team, copying assignees could introduce incorrect permissions or data exposure.

It is ambiguous whether 'excluding attachments' applies only to project-level attachments or also to task-level attachments within the task list. This could lead to inconsistent implementation and test interpretation.

There is no defined acceptance criterion for performance or completion time of the duplication operation, especially for large projects with many tasks. Without this, it is untestable whether the feature meets user experience expectations.

The requirement does not clarify whether only a Project Manager who owns or has access to the original project can duplicate it, or whether any Project Manager in the organization can duplicate any project. This is a potential access control gap.

There is no specification of what happens to project-level metadata such as project status, start date, end date, budget fields, or tags during duplication. Missing criteria make it impossible to fully validate the duplicated project's state.

The requirement ID is self-contradictory: the enclosing label is 'REQ-23' but the requirement text references 'REQ-019'. It is unclear which identifier is canonical, making traceability to test cases, defects, and other requirements unreliable.

The requirement states the system 'shall allow a Contributor' to create a task, but REQ-010 establishes three roles (Administrator, Project Manager, Contributor) with distinct permissions. It is unspecified whether Administrators and Project Managers are also permitted to create tasks, creating a potential conflict or gap in role-based access control.

No acceptance criteria are defined for what constitutes a valid or invalid task creation attempt. Missing criteria include: maximum/minimum length for title and description fields, acceptable date formats and range constraints for the due date (e.g., must be a future date), whether all four fields are mandatory or optional, and what error messages or system responses are expected on invalid input.

The 'assignee' field is ambiguous: it is unclear whether the assignee must be a member of the same project, whether multiple assignees are allowed, whether assigning to oneself is permitted, and what happens if the assignee's account is deactivated (as per REQ-011) after assignment.

There is no acceptance criterion specifying the system's response after a task is successfully created (e.g., confirmation message, redirection to task detail page, notification to the assignee). Without this, post-creation behavior cannot be verified.

The requirement does not specify whether a Contributor can create a task in any project or only in projects they are a member of. This is a security gap that could allow unauthorized task creation in projects the Contributor has no membership in.

The due date field lacks a definition of valid formats, time zone handling, or whether time-of-day precision is required. Given REQ-012 supports per-user time zone settings, it is untestable whether due dates are stored and displayed consistently across users in different time zones.

There is no specification of the minimum required fields for task creation. It cannot be determined whether the system should enforce all four fields (title, description, due date, assignee) as mandatory or allow task creation with only a subset, making pass/fail criteria for validation tests undefined.

The requirement ID is malformed — it is labeled both 'REQ-24' and 'REQ-020', creating a traceability conflict. It is unclear which ID is canonical, making it impossible to reliably reference or track this requirement across documentation, test cases, and change requests.

The phrase 'at a time' is ambiguous. It is unclear whether this means (a) only one user can be the active assignee at any given moment but history of reassignments is permitted, (b) the system must prevent concurrent assignment updates, or (c) a task can never have more than one assignee in any state. Each interpretation requires a different implementation and test approach.

No acceptance criteria are defined for what happens when an attempt is made to assign a task that is already assigned to another user. The requirement does not specify whether the system should warn the assigner, require confirmation, automatically unassign the previous user, or reject the operation.

The requirement does not specify which roles (Administrator, Project Manager, Contributor as defined in REQ-010) are permitted to assign or reassign a task. Without role-based constraints, this requirement is incomplete and potentially conflicts with the role permission model introduced in REQ-010.

There is a potential conflict with REQ-010, which states that the three roles each have 'distinct permissions'. If a Contributor is not permitted to assign tasks but this requirement says 'the system shall allow a task to be assigned', it is unclear who the actor is, creating a permission model conflict.

The requirement does not address whether a task can exist in an unassigned state. It is unclear if assignment to exactly one user is enforced at task creation time or only after an initial assignment is made. This gap makes edge-case test scenarios untestable.

The requirement does not specify behavior when the assigned user is deactivated (as covered by REQ-011). It is unclear whether the task becomes unassigned, is automatically reassigned, or remains linked to the deactivated account, creating a traceability and coverage gap.

The requirement does not define whether 'exactly one user' must be a member of the same project or organization, or whether any registered user in the system is a valid assignee. This omission is a security gap that could allow tasks to be assigned to users outside the intended project team, potentially exposing project data.

No notification or audit trail behavior is specified for assignment or reassignment events. It is untestable whether the system correctly communicates an assignment change to the newly assigned user or logs the change, as no expected outcome is defined.

The requirement ID itself is malformed and ambiguous: the outer identifier is REQ-25 while the inner content references REQ-021. It is unclear which ID is canonical, creating traceability and versioning confusion across the SRS.

No acceptance criteria are defined for the four task statuses. There is no specification of valid status transitions (e.g., can a task move directly from 'To Do' to 'Done'?), who is permitted to change status, or whether any workflow rules or guards apply between states.

The requirement does not define whether these four statuses are the only permitted statuses or whether the list is extensible. It is untestable whether a future addition of a custom status would constitute compliance or a violation.

'Support' is ambiguous language. It is unclear whether the system must enforce these statuses as the only allowed values, merely display them as defaults, allow customization, or persist them in a data store with referential integrity.

There is no specification of role-based permissions for status changes. REQ-010 establishes three roles (Administrator, Project Manager, Contributor) with distinct permissions, but REQ-021 does not clarify which roles may transition tasks between which statuses, creating a potential conflict or gap.
No acceptance criteria exist for what happens to a task's status when the assignee is deactivated (per REQ-011). The system behavior in this edge case is entirely unspecified.

There is no definition of the 'In Review' status in terms of who triggers it, what review process it implies, or what conditions must be met before transitioning out of it. This makes the status untestable beyond its mere existence in a UI dropdown.
No audit or security requirement is stated for status changes. There is no requirement to log who changed a status and when, which is a security and compliance gap for a project management tool.

The requirement ID is inconsistently labeled as both 'REQ-26' and 'REQ-022' within the same statement. It is unclear which identifier is authoritative, making traceability and cross-referencing unreliable.

The term 'organized into subtasks' is ambiguous. It is unclear whether this means a task can be broken down into subtasks at creation time only, at any point in its lifecycle, or both. The actor permitted to perform this action (any user, Project Manager, Contributor, etc.) is also unspecified.

No acceptance criteria are defined for what constitutes a 'level' of nesting. It is unclear whether the top-level task counts as level one (allowing one additional nesting level) or whether two levels of subtasks are permitted beneath the top-level task, resulting in three total tiers.

There are no acceptance criteria specifying what behavior the system shall exhibit when a user attempts to create a subtask that would exceed the maximum nesting depth of two levels (e.g., error message, UI control disablement, silent rejection).

The requirement does not specify whether subtasks inherit attributes of their parent task (e.g., assignee, due date, status, priority, project association). This omission creates ambiguity about data model behavior and makes complete testing impossible.

There is no specification of how task status and progress roll up from subtasks to parent tasks. For example, it is unclear whether completing all subtasks automatically marks the parent task as complete, which would be critical for testable verification.

The requirement does not define the behavior when a parent task is deleted — specifically whether subtasks are also deleted (cascade), reassigned to the grandparent level, or orphaned. This creates an untestable edge case.
No minimum or maximum number of subtasks per parent task is defined, making it impossible to test boundary conditions for subtask count limits.
The requirement does not specify which user roles are permitted to create, edit, reorder, or delete subtasks, creating a potential conflict with the role-based permission model defined in REQ-010.

There is no specification of whether subtasks support the same features as top-level tasks (e.g., comments, file attachments, due dates, assignments). The absence of this boundary makes the scope of testing undefined.

The requirement ID is inconsistently formatted: the section label uses 'REQ-27' while the requirement itself is identified as 'REQ-023'. This dual-ID ambiguity makes traceability unreliable and could cause confusion during testing, defect tracking, and change management.

The notification channel is not specified. It is unclear whether the notification should be delivered via email, in-app notification, push notification, SMS, or a combination. Without this, testers cannot verify the correct delivery mechanism.

No timing or latency requirement is defined for when the notification must be delivered after the assignment or reassignment event occurs. There is no SLA such as 'within X seconds/minutes', making it untestable for timeliness.

There are no acceptance criteria defining the required content or format of the notification (e.g., task name, project name, assigner identity, due date, deep-link to the task). Without this, completeness and correctness of the notification cannot be verified.
The behavior when the assignee assigns the task to themselves is not defined. It is unclear whether a self-assignment should trigger a notification, which could result in noisy or unexpected UX.
There is no specification for what happens if the notification fails to deliver (e.g., invalid email, notification service outage). Retry logic, fallback behavior, and error handling are absent.

The requirement does not address whether users can opt out of or configure assignment notifications. The absence of user preference controls may conflict with privacy regulations (e.g., GDPR, CAN-SPAM) and general usability expectations.
The behavior when a task is assigned to a deactivated user (per REQ-011) is not defined. It is unclear whether a notification attempt should be made, suppressed, or rerouted to an administrator.

The requirement does not clarify whether bulk assignments or reassignments (e.g., reassigning multiple tasks simultaneously) should trigger individual notifications per task or a consolidated digest notification, leaving notification volume behavior ambiguous.

There is no requirement covering notification behavior for role-based assignments (e.g., assigning a task to a team or role group rather than a specific individual), which may be a valid use case given the team-oriented nature of TaskFlow.

The requirement does not specify the notification channel(s) to be used (e.g., in-app notification, email, push notification, SMS). It is ambiguous whether one or all channels must be triggered, and whether user preferences for notification delivery should be respected.

No acceptance criteria are defined. Missing criteria include: maximum acceptable delay between the @mention event and notification delivery, behavior when the mentioned user is the author of the comment, behavior when the mentioned user is deactivated or does not belong to the project, whether duplicate notifications are suppressed for multiple @mentions in the same comment, and what content the notification must include (e.g., comment snippet, task link, author name).

The term 'comment' is ambiguous. TaskFlow supports comments in the context of tasks, but it is unclear whether @mention notifications also apply to comments on projects, file attachments, or other entities that may exist in the system.

There is no defined latency or timeliness requirement for the notification. Without a measurable delivery time threshold (e.g., 'within 30 seconds'), the requirement cannot be objectively tested for performance compliance.

The requirement does not address notification security or access control. A user who has been removed from a project or deactivated (see REQ-011) could potentially still receive notifications containing task or project content, representing a potential information disclosure risk.

No mention is made of how @mention resolution interacts with user roles (REQ-010). It is unclear whether a Contributor can @mention an Administrator or Project Manager outside their project scope, and whether the system should restrict or permit cross-role/cross-project mentions.

There are no acceptance criteria covering notification grouping or throttling. If a user is @mentioned many times in rapid succession, the expected system behavior (individual notifications vs. digest vs. rate-limiting) is not specified, making it untestable for robustness scenarios.

The requirement ID is inconsistent: the section header labels it REQ-31 while the body text references REQ-029. This dual/conflicting numbering makes traceability unreliable and could cause the requirement to be missed or duplicated in test planning.

The requirement does not specify which file types are permitted or prohibited. Without an allowed/blocked file type list, it is untestable whether the system correctly accepts or rejects specific formats (e.g., executables, scripts), and a security gap exists for potentially malicious uploads.
No acceptance criteria define the system behavior when a file exceeds the 25 MB limit: there is no specified error message, user notification mechanism, or rollback behavior for a failed upload.

The requirement does not define a maximum number of attachments per task or a cumulative storage limit per task, project, or organization. This omission makes capacity and boundary testing impossible.

The phrase 'allow users to attach files' does not specify which user roles (Administrator, Project Manager, Contributor) are permitted to attach, view, download, or delete attachments, creating ambiguity in permission-based test scenarios.
There is no requirement addressing virus or malware scanning of uploaded attachments before they are stored or made available to other users, representing a security gap.

The requirement does not specify whether the 25 MB limit applies to the raw file size, the encoded/compressed size after transmission, or the size after server-side processing, making the boundary condition untestable with precision.

There are no acceptance criteria covering attachment retention policy, deletion behavior (e.g., what happens to attachments when a task or project is deleted), or whether deletion is permanent or soft-deleted.

The requirement does not address access control for attachments: it is unclear whether attachments inherit task-level permissions or have independent visibility rules, leaving authorization test cases undefined.

The requirement ID is inconsistently labeled as both 'REQ-32' and 'REQ-030' within the same statement. This dual numbering creates traceability ambiguity and makes it unclear which canonical ID should be used for cross-referencing, test case mapping, and change management.

No maximum file size limit is specified for attachments. Without a defined size constraint, it is impossible to test whether the system correctly accepts or rejects files based on size, and the system may be vulnerable to storage exhaustion or denial-of-service via large file uploads.
No maximum number of attachments per task or project is defined. Acceptance criteria cannot be written or tested for attachment count limits without this boundary condition being specified.

There is no requirement for malware scanning, MIME-type validation, or content inspection of uploaded files. Accepting executable-capable formats such as DOCX, XLSX, and CSV without security controls introduces a significant attack surface (e.g., macro-based malware, CSV injection).

The requirement does not specify whether file type enforcement is based on file extension, MIME type, or actual file content (magic bytes). These three methods can produce conflicting results, making the validation behavior ambiguous and difficult to test deterministically.

No expected system behavior is defined when a user attempts to upload an unsupported file type (e.g., MP4, EXE, ZIP). Without a specified error response, message, or UI behavior, there are no acceptance criteria to validate the rejection path.

It is unspecified which roles (Administrator, Project Manager, Contributor) are permitted to upload, view, download, or delete attachments. Without role-based access criteria, permission boundaries cannot be tested and may conflict with REQ-010.

The requirement does not state whether attachments are scoped to tasks, projects, comments, or all of the above. This ambiguity makes it untestable to determine where attachment functionality should appear in the UI and which entities must support the feature.

No retention, deletion, or storage quota policy is defined for attachments. It is untestable whether the system correctly manages attachment lifecycle, including behavior when a parent task or project is deleted.

The statement 'easy and intuitive for administrators' is subjective and contains no measurable or objective criteria. This requirement is entirely untestable as written; no pass/fail condition can be derived from it.
